clusters.name: SNCC and SCLC Organizational Core
clusters.description: Senior SNCC strategists, SCLC chiefs of staff, NAACP national-office veterans, and Justice Department civil-rights attorneys whose accounts thread the inter-organizational architecture of the movement. Vocabulary leans toward COFO, Project C, MFDP credentials challenges, Citizenship Schools, Friends of SNCC fundraising, and Memphis/AFSCME labor crossover.

clusters.name: Mississippi Grassroots and Till Family Witnesses
clusters.description: Hattiesburg beauticians, Port Gibson farmers, Albany Movement workers, Bogalusa Voters League families, and the Wright cousins who shared Emmett Till's bedroom in Money, Mississippi. Geography concentrates in southern Mississippi and Louisiana with first-person testimony to local NAACP boycotts, Klan retaliation, and named federal cases like Connor v. Johnson and NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware.
clusters.starter_query: small-town Mississippi NAACP underground and Klan violence
clusters.exemplar_entry_subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
member_entry_subjects: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins

members.entry_subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins

clusters.name: SNCC Field Organizing and Direct Action
clusters.description: SNCC field secretaries and CORE freedom-riders working in Lowndes, Selma, St. Augustine, Albany, Cambridge, and Hale County who emphasize on-the-ground voter-registration drives and the discipline of nonviolent direct action. The frame is the daily organizing labor between 1961 and 1966, including the St. Augustine Four jailings, the Cambridge Maryland CNAC campaign, and SCOPE volunteer deployments.
clusters.starter_query: SNCC field secretary in Lowndes County
member_entry_subjects: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer

members.entry_subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer

clusters.name: Citizenship Education and SNCC-NSM Bridges
clusters.description: Organizers who connected SNCC field projects to Northern Student Movement, Tuskegee TIAL, SCLC's Dorchester citizenship-training program, and Black Power-era publishing and academia. The cluster centers on movement education infrastructure, with vocabulary around Jonathan Daniels and Hayneville, the Dorchester Academy curriculum, CORE Western Region in San Diego, and the formation of Africana Studies programs.
member_entry_subjects: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman

members.entry_subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman

clusters.name: SNCC Women Organizers and Freedom Summer
clusters.description: Women organizers across SNCC's Greenwood, Selma, Laurel, Albany, and Lowndes projects whose work spanned Freedom Summer 1964 and the Mississippi State Fairgrounds detentions to later Black Panther chapters in the North. The cluster anchors on Hands on the Freedom Plow contributors, the Pearlena Lewis Woolworth's sit-in beatings, NAACP Youth Council Jackson walkouts, and the Pike County voter-registration project.
member_entry_subjects: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)

members.entry_subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)

clusters.name: Mississippi Delta Voter Registration
clusters.description: Field workers and local witnesses from the Greenwood, Itta Bena, Leflore, and Holmes County voter-registration projects and adjacent Albany Movement church-burning testimony. Vocabulary leans on Fannie Lou Hamer's Winona beating, McComb Freedom Houses bombed during 1964, Bob Moses-led literacy classes, and Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Terrell County burned by Klansmen.
clusters.starter_query: Winona jail Fannie Lou Hamer beating

clusters.name: Howard NAG and Black Power Transition
clusters.description: Howard University Nonviolent Action Group founders and SNCC organizers who moved the organization through its 1966-67 Black Power transition into the Sixth Pan-African Congress, the Black Panther Party leadership, and the Newark Black United Front. The frame is the post-1965 ideological turn toward Pan-Africanism, Drum and Spear Bookstore, Newark community organizing, and Stokely Carmichael's chairmanship.

clusters.name: Black Panther Party Chapters
clusters.description: Black Panther Party rank-and-file and chapter leadership in Seattle, Oakland, Los Angeles, and Chicago who built free breakfast programs, free clinics, and community schools while navigating the Newton-Cleaver split and COINTELPRO surveillance. Anchored on the Dixon brothers' Seattle chapter, the Bunchy Carter and George Jackson clinics, Fred Hampton's Illinois education cadre, and the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Lords.

clusters.name: Women in NAACP and California Civil Rights
clusters.description: A Virginia Union sit-in participant, an LAUSD counselor arrested at Thalhimer's, a Los Angeles CORE tester of the Unruh Civil Rights Act, and Thurgood Marshall's wife from the NAACP national office. Connecting threads are Brown v. Board generation litigation support, Black children's-book authorship, California fair-housing organizing, and the Richmond, Virginia Thalhimer's department-store sit-ins.

clusters.name: Movement-Trained Academics and Educators
clusters.description: Birmingham Children's Crusade jailees, Greensboro Bennett College sit-inners, USM administrator who first integrated the university, and an LDF attorney's son who desegregated Richmond's schools, all of whom became higher-education leaders. The cluster centers on the long arc from childhood movement participation into university presidencies, Africana Studies departments, and Bob Moses's Algebra Project.

clusters.name: Northern Allies and Interracial Organizing
clusters.description: A SNCC field worker in Arkansas, a Tougaloo professor co-founder of the Free Southern Theater, an interracial 1940s couple in the Rustin-Randolph orbit, and a Philadelphia movement educator. Threads include the 1957 Prayer Pilgrimage for Freedom, Northern movement support networks, the 1966 SNCC vote on white organizers in Black communities, and movement-era arts and photography collectives.
member_entry_subjects: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett

members.entry_subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett

clusters.name: Charleston Hospital Strike and Corporate Desegregation
clusters.description: Three organizers whose accounts focus on labor-rights and corporate-integration negotiations rather than student direct action: the 1969 Charleston Hospital Workers Strike, the Woolworth's lunch-counter corporate negotiation, and the St. Augustine ACCORD Freedom Trail. The shared frame is institutional-channel civil-rights work outside the SNCC and SCLC field-organizing template.

clusters.name: Highlander Folk School and Freedom Songs
clusters.description: Music director and program director at Highlander Folk School, folk musician Pete Seeger, and a Montgomery NAACP Youth Council walker who credits Highlander with the We Are Not Afraid verse of We Shall Overcome. Anchored on the 1957 Highlander anniversary, Zilphia Horton's song archive, and the Montgomery Gospel Trio.
clusters.exemplar_entry_subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
member_entry_subjects: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan

members.entry_subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voices (King Counsel, Albany SNCC, Athlete-Witness)
clusters.description: A King speechwriter and attorney, an Albany Movement SNCC field worker, and Boston Celtics center Bill Russell, three voices that did not cluster strongly with the main field-organizing or legal-strategy groupings. The shared geometry is biographical singularity rather than a single thematic anchor; useful for users seeking outlier perspectives on King's inner circle, southwest Georgia organizing, and athletic-celebrity witness.
clusters.starter_query: Martin Luther King speechwriter Clarence B. Jones

clusters.name: Baptist Clergy in the Movement
clusters.description: Baptist ministers in Jackson, Shreveport, and Seattle whose pulpits anchored civil-rights organizing, including the first Mississippi NAACP Youth Council under Medgar Evers, the 1963 beating at Little Union Baptist Church after the 16th Street bombing, and Dr. King's 1961 Seattle visit. Vocabulary leans on Morehouse seminary training, mass-meeting pastoring, and SCLC field staff.
clusters.starter_query: Baptist church mass meeting civil rights

clusters.name: Brown Berets and Pan-Ethnic Coalitions
clusters.description: A Tongva-elder Brown Berets co-founder in East Los Angeles and a Sankofa-influenced Black filmmaker from Leimert Park, two voices outside the Black civil-rights mainstream that share Los Angeles geography and pan-ethnic coalition vocabulary. The frame is the late-1960s Brown Berets free clinic, the La Piranya Coffee House, and Project Blowed hip-hop pedagogy.

clusters.name: Hattiesburg and Baker County Family Violence
clusters.description: A Hattiesburg NAACP voter-registration applicant under registrar Theron Lynd and the widow of Hosie Miller (Shirley Sherrod's father), shot in the back in Baker County, Georgia in 1965. Both anchor on rural Mississippi and Georgia voter-registration retaliation and the long-tail of family-level violence in the Deep South.

clusters.name: Head Start and Rural Community Development
clusters.description: A Holmes County, Mississippi CDGM Head Start worker and a Marvell, Arkansas community-development center founder, both of whom directed federal-funded child-development programs across the rural Delta. Vocabulary centers on the Child Development Group of Mississippi, Senator John Stennis's defunding pressure, on-site Medical Committee clinics, and Boys, Girls, and Adults Community Development Center programs.

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voices (Klan-Murder Family, Black General)
clusters.description: The son of NAACP martyr Vernon Dahmer Sr. and Lieutenant General Julius Becton, two biographically singular voices that the geometry placed together despite distinct subjects (Klan firebombing prosecution thirty years later versus career-military integration). Useful for users seeking Hattiesburg movement aftermath or pioneering Black military leadership separately.

clusters.name: Medical Committee for Human Rights
clusters.description: Two of the Medical Committee for Human Rights co-founding physicians who deployed to Mississippi during Freedom Summer 1964 and Bolivar County in 1965-66 to establish the Mound Bayou community health center. Anchored on the recovery of the Goodman-Chaney-Schwerner bodies, the OEO grant for Tufts-Delta, and the community-oriented primary-care model.
clusters.starter_query: Medical Committee for Human Rights Mississippi

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voices (BPP Founder, Chicano Federation)
clusters.description: Black Panther Party co-founder Big Man Howard and San Diego Chicano organizer Mateo Camarillo, voices the geometry paired but whose work spans different movement frames (national BPP founding versus West Coast pan-ethnic labor and immigration organizing). The shared thread is West Coast multi-ethnic civil rights, though the testimonies cover distinct organizational substrates.

clusters.name: Clara Luper Family and Oklahoma City Sit-Ins
clusters.description: Two children of NAACP Youth Council leader Clara Luper recounting the 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-ins in Oklahoma City, among the earliest organized lunch-counter sit-ins of the modern movement, predating Greensboro by sixteen months. Anchored on Clara Luper's 26 arrests, the 1957 NAACP Youth Council Northern tour, and the 1969 Oklahoma City Sanitation Workers Strike.
clusters.starter_query: Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-in 1958

clusters.name: Black Agricultural Land Trusts and Rural Co-Ops
clusters.description: Federation of Southern Cooperatives organizer who co-founded New Communities Inc. (a Black agricultural land trust) and a partner from a similar rural-development cooperative. The frame is Black landownership preservation, the Pigford v. Glickman USDA discrimination settlement, and post-Movement rural economic infrastructure.
clusters.starter_query: Federation of Southern Cooperatives land trust

clusters.name: Sunflower and Holmes County MFDP
clusters.description: SNCC field secretary in Sunflower County who drove Fannie Lou Hamer to her first registration attempt, and a Holmes County Durant Movement leader who chaired the MFDP delegate process for Atlantic City. Anchored on the 1966 Meredith March Black Power declaration, the Mileston Fourteen, and the firebombing of Hartman Turnbow's house.

clusters.name: South Carolina Civil Rights Attorneys
clusters.description: Two LDF cooperating-counsel attorneys in South Carolina who tried hundreds of segregation cases, defended Friendship Junior College sit-in students, represented Orangeburg Massacre protesters, and went on to the federal and state high benches. Geography anchors on Sumter, Rock Hill, Columbia, and Judge J. Waties Waring's courtroom.
clusters.starter_query: South Carolina segregation litigation Briggs Elliott

clusters.name: NAACP Legal Defense Fund Strategy
clusters.description: Two LDF director-counsel-level attorneys (Carter and Greenberg) whose accounts trace the 1950 strategic shift from equalization suits to a direct attack on Plessy v. Ferguson, the Brown v. Board litigation strategy, and the post-Brown Title VII employment-discrimination era. Vocabulary centers on McLaurin v. Oklahoma, Topeka, and the LDF training program for Black lawyers.
clusters.starter_query: Brown v. Board LDF litigation strategy

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voice (Joan Trumpauer Mulholland)
clusters.description: A single-member cluster: Arlington, Virginia native Joan Trumpauer Mulholland, a white NAG organizer at Howard who joined the May-June 1961 Freedom Rides into Jackson and was incarcerated at Parchman Farm. Her testimony's geometry placed her alone, likely because her white-Southern, deeply-embedded Tougaloo trajectory does not closely match either the NAG-Howard or the Mississippi-grassroots cluster centroids.

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voice (Sam Young Jr.)
clusters.description: A single-member cluster: Worth County, Georgia organizer Sam Young, Jr., active in the Southwest Georgia Project under Charles Sherrod during the freedom-of-choice school-integration period. His testimony falls between the Albany Movement and the SNCC field-organizing centroids without clustering strongly with either.

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voice (Maynard E. Moore)
clusters.description: A single-member cluster: Petersburg, Virginia Methodist who became active in the Methodist Student Movement and was elected to statewide office as a high-school senior. His denominational-organizing frame and Methodist Episcopal Church (South) family history did not match any cluster centroid.

clusters.name: Distinct Individual Voice (Richard Barry Sobol)
clusters.description: A single-member cluster: New Orleans civil-rights attorney with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee who litigated Title VII discrimination cases against Crown Zellerbach in Bogalusa and partnered with Collins, Douglas, and Elie. His specialized federal-litigation track did not cluster with either the LDF-strategy attorneys or the South Carolina state-court attorneys.
